title: Bincount
description:
Given an integer vector $a\in\{0,\dots,j-1\}^n$, return a length-$j$ vector whose $p$-th entry counts how many elements of $a$ equal $p$.

**Shapes**: `a`:[n] -> output:[j].
starter_code:
import torch

def bincount(a, j):
    # a: [n] ints in [0, j); return length-j counts of each value
    # TODO
    pass
hints: Build a `[j, n]` boolean matrix comparing every candidate bucket value `arange(j)` against every element of `a`.
Matrix-multiplying that boolean matrix by a length-n vector of ones sums each row without using `sum` directly — 1 line, broadcasting only.

solutions.name: Broadcast equality + matmul sum
solutions.explanation: `arange(j)[:, None] == a[None, :]` gives a `[j, n]` matrix that is $1$ at $(p,k)$ exactly when $a_k=p$. Matmuling with a vector of ones sums each row, i.e. counts how many elements of `a` equal each bucket $p$, avoiding the banned `sum`/`cumsum` primitives.
